glossary.description: A facility composed of networked computers and storage.

glossary.name: dCloud
glossary.description: dCloud allows you to have access to a broad list of pre-scripted demos that are fully supported, from 9 different Cisco architectures (collaboration, enterprise network, service provider, security, data center virtualization, and more.

glossary.name: DCN
glossary.description: Cisco Data Center Networking (DCN) software brings automation and policy-driven management and control to your data center. Cisco® DCN licensing provides flexibility when adopting various software consumption models.

glossary.name: EC2
glossary.description: Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) is a web service that provides secure, resizable compute capacity in the cloud. It is designed to make web-scale cloud computing easier for developers. Amazon EC2 is a simple web service interface that allows you to obtain and configure capacity with minimal friction.

glossary.name: Edge
glossary.description: The word edge in this context means literal geographic distribution. Edge computing is computing that is done at or near the source of the data, instead of relying on the cloud at one of a dozen data centers to do all the work.

glossary.name: Elastic IP
glossary.description: An Elastic IP address is a reserved public IP address that you can assign to any EC2 instance in a particular region, until you choose to release it.

glossary.name: EPG
glossary.description: ACI Endpoint Groups (EPGs) provide a new model for mapping applications to the network. EPGs act as a container for collections of applications, or application components and tiers that can be used to apply forwarding and policy logic.

glossary.name: ERP
glossary.description: Enterprise Resource Planning. ERP is usually referred to as a category of business management software—typically a suite of integrated applications—that an organization can use to collect, store, manage, and interpret data from many business activities.

glossary.name: ESXi
glossary.description: ESXi stands for "ESX integrated". VMware ESXi originated as a compact version of VMware ESX that allowed for a smaller 32 MB disk footprint on the host.

glossary.name: FaaS
glossary.description: Function as a service is a category of cloud computing services that provides a platform allowing customers to develop, run, and manage application functionalities without the complexity of building and maintaining the infrastructure typically associated with developing and launching an application.

glossary.name: Fabric
glossary.description: Data fabric is an architecture and set of data services that provide consistent capabilities across a choice of endpoints spanning on-premises and multiple cloud environments. Data fabric simplifies and integrates data management across cloud and on premises to accelerate digital transformation.

glossary.name: Fabric Interconnects
glossary.description: The Unified Computing System (UCS) Fabric Interconnects is a networking switch or head unit where the UCS chassis, essentially a rack where server components are attached, connects to. The UCS fabric interconnect is a core part of Cisco’s Unified Computing System, which is designed to improve scalability and reduce the total cost of ownership of data centers by integrating all components into a single platform, which acts as a single unit. Access to networks and storage is then provided through the UCS fabric interconnect.

glossary.name: FCAPS
glossary.description: FCAPS is the ISO Telecommunications Management Network model and framework for network management. FCAPS is an acronym for fault, configuration, accounting, performance, security, the management categories into which the ISO model defines network management tasks.

glossary.name: FCoE
glossary.description: Fibre Channel over Ethernet (FCoE) is a computer network technology that encapsulates Fibre Channel frames over Ethernet networks. This allows Fibre Channel to use 10 Gigabit Ethernet networks (or higher speeds) while preserving the Fibre Channel protocol.

glossary.name: Flash Stack
glossary.description: A converged infrastructure partnership between Cisco and Pure Storage, in order to integrate Pure Storage flash storage devices with Cisco blade servers.

glossary.name: FlexPod
glossary.description: FlexPod is a Converged Infrastructure (CI) made up of a defined set of hardware and software from Cisco and NetApp that forms an integrated system foundation for both virtualized, containerized, and baremetal solutions. FlexPod combines NetApp storage, Cisco Nexus networking, Cisco Unified Computing System (Cisco UCS), and VMware vSphere software in a single package.

glossary.name: Full Stack
glossary.description: A solution stack or software stack is a set of software subsystems or components needed to create a complete platform such that no additional software is needed to support applications. Applications are said to run on or run on top of the resulting platform… For example, to develop a web application the architect defines the stack as the target operating system, web server, database, and programming language. Another version of a software stack is operating system, middleware, database, and applications. Regularly, the components of a software stack are developed by different developers independently from one another. A full-stack developer is expected to be able to work in all the layers of the stack.

glossary.name: Gartner MQ
glossary.description: Gartner Magic Quadrant. Magic Quadrant is a series of market research reports published by IT consulting firm Gartner that rely on proprietary qualitative data analysis methods to demonstrate market trends, such as direction, maturity and participants.

glossary.name: GENEVE
glossary.description: Generic Network Virtualization Encapsulation… promises to address the perceived limitations of the earlier specifications and support all of the capabilities of VXLAN, NVGRE and STT.

glossary.name: Greenfield
glossary.description: In many disciplines a greenfield project is one that lacks constraints imposed by prior work. The analogy is to that of construction on greenfield land where there is no need to work within the constraints of existing buildings or infrastructure.

glossary.name: GSP
glossary.description: Global Service Profile configured in Cisco UCS Central.

glossary.name: GVE
glossary.description: Global Virtual Engineering.

glossary.name: Hardware Support Manager (HSM)
glossary.description: HSM is a service option that allows you to manage the server firmware outside of Cisco Intersight application from VMware vCenter.

glossary.name: HashiCorp
glossary.description: HashiCorp is a software company with a Freemium business model based in San Francisco, California. HashiCorp provides open-source tools and commercial products that enable developers, operators and security professionals to provision, secure, run and connect cloud-computing infrastructure.

glossary.name: Helm Chart
glossary.description: Helm uses a packaging format called charts. A chart is a collection of files that describe a related set of Kubernetes resources. A single chart might be used to deploy something simple, like a memcached pod, or something complex, like a full web app stack with HTTP servers, databases, caches, and so on.

glossary.name: HX CSI
glossary.description: Cisco HyperFlex Container Storage Interface (CSI) is an out-of-tree container-based Kubernetes storage integration; which is deployed and consumed through standard Kubernetes primitives such as Persistent Volume Claims and Storage Classes. Cisco HyperFlex CSI supports the following features: Dynamic creation and deletion of volumes; and Dynamic volume attach and detach.

glossary.name: HX Edge Node
glossary.description: HX-E-220M5SX Edge servers extend the capabilities of Cisco’s HyperFlex portfolio in a 1U form factor with the addition of the Intel® Xeon® Processor Scalable Family, 24 DIMM slots with configuration options ranging from 128GB up to 3TB of DRAM, and a combination of SSD cache and high performance capacity HDDs for highly available, high performance storage.

glossary.name: Hybrid Cloud
glossary.description: Hybrid Cloud is a solution that combines a private cloud with one or more public cloud services, with proprietary software enabling communication between each distinct service. A hybrid cloud strategy provides businesses with greater flexibility by moving workloads between cloud solutions as needs and costs fluctuate… Hybrid cloud services are powerful because they give businesses greater control over their private data. An organization can store sensitive data on a private cloud or local data center and simultaneously leverage the robust computational resources of a managed public cloud. A hybrid cloud relies on a single plane of management, unlike a multi-cloud strategy wherein admins must manage each cloud environment separately.

glossary.name: IaaS
glossary.description: Infrastructure as a service (IaaS) is an instant computing infrastructure, provisioned and managed over the internet. It’s one of the four types of cloud services, along with software as a service (SaaS), platform as a service (PaaS), and serverless.

glossary.name: IMC
glossary.description: The Cisco® Integrated Management Controller (IMC) is a baseboard management controller that provides embedded server management for Cisco UCS® C-Series Rack Servers and Cisco UCS S-Series Storage Servers. The Cisco IMC enables system management in the data center and across distributed branch-office locations. It supports multiple management interfaces, including a Web User Interface (Web UI), a Command-Line Interface (CLI), and an XML API that is consistent with the one used by Cisco UCS Manager. IMC also supports industry-standard management protocols, including Redfish, Simple Network Management Protocol Version 3 (SNMPv3), and Intelligent Platform Management Interface Version 2.0 (IPMIv2.0).

glossary.name: IMM
glossary.description: Intersight Managed Mode (IMM) is a new architecture that manages the UCS Fabric Interconnected systems through a Redfish-based standard model. Intersight Managed Mode unifies the capabilities of the UCS Systems and the cloud-based flexibility of Intersight, thus unifying the management experience for the standalone JSON—and Fabric Interconnect attached systems. Intersight Management Model standardizes policy and operation management for the 4GFI and the Cisco UCS M5 servers.

glossary.name: Integrated Systems
glossary.description: Cisco Intersight Integrated Systems features enable the management of Converged Infrastructure (CI) in Cisco Intersight.

glossary.name: Intel VMD
glossary.description: Intel® Volume Management Device. Intel® VMD enables hot swap replacement of NVMe SSDs from the PCIe bus without shutting down the system while standardized LED management helps provide quick identification of SSD status. This commonality brings enterprise reliability, availability, and serviceability (RAS) features to NVMe SSDs, enabling you to deploy next-generation storage with confidence.

glossary.name: Intelligent Workload Placement
glossary.description: Intelligent Workload Placement determines the best destination to run your workload by rebalancing existing workloads inside a container.

glossary.name: Intersight Assist
glossary.description: Cisco Intersight Assist helps you add endpoint devices to Cisco Intersight.

glossary.name: Intersight Cloud Orchestrator
glossary.description: It is a framework to create and execute complex workflows in Intersight. A central workflow engine sequences the steps in workflows and automates the execution of workflows.

glossary.name: IOM
glossary.description: Input/Output Module

glossary.name: iSCSI
glossary.description: Internet Small Computer Systems Interface(iSCSI) is a transport layer protocol that works on top of the Transport Control Protocol (TCP). It enables block-level SCSI data transport between the iSCSI initiator and the storage target over TCP/IP networks.

glossary.name: IWO
glossary.description: Cisco Intersight Workload Optimizer

glossary.name: JSON
glossary.description: JSON is an open standard file format, and data interchange format, that uses human-readable text to store and transmit data objects consisting of attribute–value pairs and array data types. It is a very common data format, with a diverse range of applications, such as serving as a replacement for XML in AJAX systems.

glossary.name: K8s
glossary.description: Kubernetes is an orchestration tool for containers and applications. It allows users to perform this orchestration more efficiently than other tools have done in the past.

glossary.name: Kafka
glossary.description: Apache Kafka is an open-source stream-processing software platform developed by the Apache Software Foundation, written in Scala and Java. The project aims to provide a unified, high-throughput, low-latency platform for handling real-time data feeds.

glossary.name: Kernel
glossary.description: The kernel is a computer program at the core of computers operating systems that has complete control over everything in the system. It is the "portion of the operating system code that is always resident in memory",[2] and facilitates interactions between hardware and software components. On most systems, the kernel is one of the first programs loaded on startup (after the boot loader). It handles the rest of startup as well as memory, peripherals, and input/output (I/O) requests from software, translating them into data-processing instructions for the central processing unit.

glossary.name: Kubernetes
glossary.description: Kubernetes is open-source orchestration software for deploying, managing, and scaling containers. Modern applications are increasingly built using containers, which are microservices packaged with their dependencies and configurations. Kubernetes (pronounced “koo-ber-net-ees”) is open-source software for deploying and managing those containers at scale—and it’s also the Greek word for helmsmen of a ship or pilot. Build, deliver, and scale containerized apps faster with Kubernetes, sometimes referred to as “k8s” or “k-eights.”

glossary.name: LDAP
glossary.description: The Lightweight Directory Access Protocol is an open, vendor-neutral, industry standard application protocol for accessing and maintaining distributed directory information services over an Internet Protocol (IP) network. Directory services play an important role in developing intranet and Internet applications by allowing the sharing of information about users, systems, networks, services, and applications throughout the network. As examples, directory services may provide any organized set of records, often with a hierarchical structure, such as a corporate email directory. Similarly, a telephone directory is a list of subscribers with an address and a phone number.

glossary.name: Leaves
glossary.description: A spine-leaf architecture is an increasingly popular data center network topology that consists of two switching layers—a spine and leaf. Spine switches interconnect all of the leaf switches in a full-mesh topology.

glossary.name: Lob Application APIs
glossary.description: APIs developed for internal apps, or apps specific for a school, business, or industry.

glossary.name: LUN
glossary.description: In computer storage, a logical unit number, or LUN, is a number used to identify a logical unit, which is a device addressed by the SCSI protocol or by Storage Area Network protocols that encapsulate SCSI, such as Fibre Channel or iSCSI.

glossary.name: Microservice
glossary.description: Microservice architecture, or simply microservices, is a distinctive method of developing software systems that tries to focus on building single-function modules with well-defined interfaces and operations. The trend has grown popular in recent years as Enterprises look to become more Agile and move towards a DevOps and continuous testing.

glossary.name: MIT
glossary.description: Management Information Tree

glossary.name: MSO
glossary.description: Cisco Multi-Site Orchestrator (MSO) is the intersite policy manager. It provides single-pane management, enabling you to monitor the health-score state for all the interconnected sites. It also allows you to define, in a centralized place, all the Intersight policies that can then be pushed to the different APIC domains for rendering them on the physical switches building those fabrics. It thus provides a high degree of control over when and where to push those policies, hence allowing the tenant change domain separation that uniquely characterizes the Cisco ACI Multi-Site architecture.

glossary.name: Multi-cloud
glossary.description: Multi-cloud is a term for the use of more than one public cloud service provider for virtual data storage or computing power resources, with or without any existing private cloud and on-premises infrastructure. A multi-cloud strategy not only provides more flexibility for which cloud services an enterprise chooses to use, it also reduces dependence on just one cloud vendor.

glossary.name: Net New
glossary.description: A true and final result, after more than the obvious subtractions and allowances.

glossary.name: Network Assurance Engine
glossary.description:
The Cisco® Network Assurance Engine solution provides continuous verification and analysis of the entire data center network, giving operators confidence that their network is operating consistently with their intent. It combines mathematically accurate network models with codified Cisco domain knowledge to generate “smart events” that pinpoint deviations from intent and offer recommendations for remediation.

Using comprehensive analysis spanning operator intent, controller policy, switch configurations, and data-plane state, Cisco Network Assurance Engine (NAE) helps operators proactively detect network outages and security policy vulnerabilities before they impact business, reduces risk by predicting change impact, and rapidly determines the root cause of problems. With a unified network repository and compliance rules, Network Assurance Engine simplifies audits and ensures compliance. Source: Cisco Network Assurance Engine Data Sheet

glossary.name: Nexus
glossary.description: The Cisco Nexus series switches are modular and fixed port network switches designed for the data center. Cisco Systems introduced the Nexus Series of switches on January 28, 2008. All switches in the Nexus range run the modular NX-OS firmware/operating system on the fabric.

glossary.name: Nexus 9K
glossary.description: The Nexus 9000 series is a range of many models 2U to 21U rack-switches offering 60 to 2304 interfaces running on 100Mb, 1Gb, 10Gb, 25Gb, 40Gb, 100Gb, 400Gb Ethernet, 10/25/40 Gb FCoE interfaces.

glossary.name: Nexus 9K
glossary.description: The Nexus 9000 series is a range of many models 2U to 21U rack-switches offering 60 to 2304 interfaces running on 100Mb, 1Gb, 10Gb, 25Gb, 40Gb, 100Gb, 400Gb Ethernet, 10/25/40 Gb FCoE interfaces.

glossary.name: Nexus Dashboard
glossary.description: Cisco Nexus Dashboard (ND) is a single-pane-of-glass console that streamlines data center network operations and management.

glossary.name: NIC
glossary.description: A network interface controller (NIC) also known as a network interface card, network adapter, LAN adapter or physical network interface, and by similar terms) is a computer hardware component that connects a computer to a computer network.

glossary.name: Nodes
glossary.description:
In data communication, a physical network node may either be data communication equipment (DCE) such as a modem, hub, bridge or switch; or data terminal equipment (DTE) such as a digital telephone handset, a printer or a host computer.

If the network in question is a local area network (LAN) or wide area network (WAN), every LAN or WAN node that participates on the data link layer must have a network address, typically one for each network interface controller it possesses. Examples are computers, a DSL modem with Ethernet interface and wireless access point. Equipment, such as an Ethernet hub or modem with serial interface, that operates only below the data link layer does not require a network address.

If the network in question is the Internet or an intranet, many physical network nodes are host computers, also known as Internet nodes, identified by an IP address, and all hosts are physical network nodes. However, some data-link-layer devices such as switches, bridges and wireless access points do not have an IP host address (except sometimes for administrative purposes), and are not considered to be Internet nodes or hosts, but are considered physical network nodes and LAN nodes.

glossary.name: NVGRE
glossary.description: Network Virtualization using Generic Routing Encapsulation (NVGRE) is a network virtualization technology that attempts to alleviate the scalability problems associated with large cloud computing deployments. It uses Generic Routing Encapsulation (GRE) to tunnel layer 2 packets over layer 3 networks.

glossary.name: NVMe
glossary.description: NVM Express (NVMe) or Non-Volatile Memory Host Controller Interface Specification (NVMHCIS) is an open logical-device interface specification for accessing non-volatile storage media attached via PCI Express (PCIe) bus. The acronym NVM stands for non-volatile memory, which is often NAND flash memory that comes in several physical form factors, including solid-state drives (SSDs), PCI Express (PCIe) add-in cards, M.2 cards, and other forms. NVM Express, as a logical-device interface, has been designed to capitalize on the low latency and internal parallelism of solid-state storage devices.

glossary.name: NXOS
glossary.description: NX-OS mode means the switch is in standalone mode.

glossary.name: OAS
glossary.description: OpenAPI Specification—The OpenAPI Specification (OAS) defines a standard, programming language-agnostic interface description for HTTP APIs, which allows both humans and computers to discover and understand the capabilities of a service without requiring access to source code, additional documentation, or inspection of network traffic.

glossary.name: OpenStack API
glossary.description: OpenStack is the most popular open source solution for cloud data center management in enterprise. OpenStack was originally developed as a partnership between RackSpace & NASA. Business organizations, academic researchers, or other groups can install OpenStack to orchestrate data center operations on bare metal, private cloud hardware, public cloud resources, or both (hybrid/multi-cloud architecture). OpenStack includes advance use of virtualization & SDN for network traffic optimization.

glossary.name: Orchestration
glossary.description: In system administration, orchestration is the automated configuration, coordination, and management of computer systems and software. A number of tools exist for automation of server configuration and management, including Ansible, Puppet, Salt, Terraform, and AWS CloudFormation.

glossary.name: PaaS
glossary.description: Platform as a service (PaaS) is a complete development and deployment environment in the cloud, with resources that enable you to deliver everything from simple cloud-based apps to sophisticated, cloud-enabled enterprise applications. You purchase the resources you need from a cloud service provider on a pay-as-you-go basis and access them over a secure Internet connection

glossary.name: PCNS
glossary.description: PowerChute Network Shutdown

glossary.name: PID
glossary.description: Product ID

glossary.name: PIE
glossary.description: Package Installation Envelope

glossary.name: Pods
glossary.description: The Cisco ® Application Centric Infrastructure (Cisco ACI ™) Multi-Pod solution is an evolution of the stretched-fabric use case. Although, each pod consists of its own spine and leaf switches, all the pods reside within the same fabric and are managed by a single APIC cluster.

glossary.name: Pools
glossary.description: A collection of resources that are kept ready to use, rather than acquired on use and released afterwards. In this context, resources can refer to system resources such as file handles, which are external to a process, or internal resources such as objects. A pool client requests a resource from the pool and performs desired operations on the returned resource. When the client finishes its use of the resource, it is returned to the pool rather than released and lost.

glossary.name: Private Cloud
glossary.description: The private cloud is defined as computing services offered either over the Internet or a private internal network and only to select users instead of the general public.

glossary.name: Private Key
glossary.description: A private key, also known as a secret key, is a variable in cryptography that is used with an algorithm to encrypt and decrypt code. Secret keys are only shared with the key generator, making it highly secure. Private keys play an important role in symmetric cryptography, asymmetric cryptography and cryptocurrencies.

glossary.name: Public Cloud
glossary.description: Public cloud is an IT model where on-demand computing services and infrastructure are managed by a third-party provider and shared with multiple organizations using the public Internet. Public cloud service providers may offer cloud-based services such as infrastructure as a service (IaaS), platform as a service (PaaS), or software as a service (Saas) to users for either a monthly or pay-per-use fee, eliminating the need for users to host these services on site in their own data center.

glossary.name: Public key
glossary.description: A public key is a large numerical value that is used to encrypt data. The key can be generated by a software program, but more often, it is provided by a trusted, designated authority and made available to everyone through a publicly accessible repository or directory.

glossary.name: QoS
glossary.description: Quality of service (QoS) refers to any technology that manages data traffic to reduce packet loss, latency and jitter on a network. QoS controls and manages network resources by setting priorities for specific types of data on the network.

glossary.name: Rack Server
glossary.description: A rack server, or rack-mounted server, is any server that is built specifically to be mounted within a server rack. Rack servers are a general-purpose machine that can be configured to support a wide range of requirements. They are most commonly found in data center environments but can also be used in smaller computer closets. Unlike traditional servers that look much like a PC, a rack server is wider. So it can be secured into the rack using mounting screws or rails, depending on the design. If you only require a small number of servers, they are the best choice economically due to the lower upfront costs.

glossary.name: RAS
glossary.description: In computer memory technology, RAS (row address strobe) is a signal sent to a dynamic random access memory (DRAM) that tells it that an associated address is a row address. A data bit in DRAM is stored in a cell located by the intersection of a column address and a row address.

glossary.name: RBAC
glossary.description: Role-based access control

glossary.name: RDM
glossary.description: Raw device mapping

glossary.name: Redfish
glossary.description: The Redfish standard is a suite of specifications that deliver an industry standard protocol providing a RESTful interface for the management of servers, storage, networking, and converged infrastructure.

glossary.name: RESTful API (REST API)
glossary.description:
A RESTful API is an architectural style for an application program interface (API) that uses HTTP requests to access and use data. That data can be used to GET, PUT, POST and DELETE data types, which refers to the reading, updating, creating and deleting of operations concerning resources.

An API for a website is code that allows two software programs to communicate with each other. The API spells out the proper way for a developer to write a program requesting services from an operating system or other application.

A RESTful API -- also referred to as a RESTful web service or REST API -- is based on representational state transfer (REST), which is an architectural style and approach to communications often used in web services development.

glossary.name: ROBO
glossary.description: A remote office/branch office (ROBO) is an office that is located in a different or remote geographical area. An organization may have a main office, as well as remote offices in another city, country or continent… ROBO data system storage and recovery is a common and problematic organizational issue.

glossary.name: Root cause
glossary.description: A root cause is an initiating cause of either a condition or a causal chain that leads to an outcome or effect of interest. The term denotes the earliest, most basic, deepest, cause for a given behavior; most often a fault. The idea is that you can only see an error by its manifest signs. Wikipedia

glossary.name: SaaS
glossary.description: Software as a service (SaaS) allows users to connect to and use cloud-based apps over the Internet. Common examples are email, calendaring, and office tools (such as Microsoft Office 365).

glossary.name: SAML
glossary.description: Security Assertion Markup Language is an open standard for exchanging authentication and authorization data between parties, in particular, between an identity provider and a service provider. SAML is an XML-based markup language for security assertions.

glossary.name: SAN
glossary.description: A storage area network (SAN) or storage network is a computer network which provides access to consolidated, block-level data storage. SANs are primarily used to access storage devices, such as disk arrays and tape libraries from servers so that the devices appear to the operating system as direct-attached storage.

glossary.name: Scalability
glossary.description: Scalability is the measure of the ability of a system to increase or decrease in performance and cost in response to changes in application and system processing demands. Examples would include how well a hardware system performs when the number of users are increased, how well a database withstands growing numbers of queries, or how well an operating system performs on different classes of hardware. Enterprises that are growing rapidly should pay special attention to scalability when evaluating hardware and software.

glossary.name: Secure Boot attestation
glossary.description: Part of HyperFlex, this verifies that the boot process has been done securely.

glossary.name: SecureX
glossary.description: SecureX is a cloud-native, built-in platform experience that connects the Cisco Secure portfolio and your infrastructure. It is integrated and open for simplicity. For more information, see Cisco SecureX.

glossary.name: Server virtualization
glossary.description: Server virtualization is the process of dividing a physical server into multiple unique and isolated virtual servers by means of a software application. Each virtual server can run its own operating systems independently.

glossary.name: SFF
glossary.description: Small form factor. Small form factor storage servers contain 2.5 inch drives.

glossary.name: Single pane of glass experience
glossary.description: Single pane of glass is a tool used in IT and management fields that unifies data or interfaces across several sources and presents them in a single view.

glossary.name: Slipstreaming
glossary.description: In computing, slipstreaming is the act of integrating patches (including service packs) into the installation files of their original app, so that the result allows a direct installation of the updated app.

glossary.name: SMM
glossary.description: The Cisco Intersight Service Mesh Manager (Intersight SMM) extension, is a managed Istio offering. It enables you to observe, inspect, and manage application traffic, control the ingress/egress gateways, and use policy-based secure connectivity.

glossary.name: SOAP
glossary.description: SOAP (formerly an acronym for Simple Object Access Protocol) is a messaging protocol specification for exchanging structured information in the implementation of web services in computer networks. Its purpose is to provide extensibility, neutrality, verbosity and independence. [vague] It uses XML Information Set for its message format, and relies on application layer protocols, most often Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP), although some legacy systems communicate over Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP), for message negotiation and transmission. .

glossary.name: SOAP
glossary.description: SOAP (formerly an acronym for Simple Object Access Protocol) is a messaging protocol specification for exchanging structured information in the implementation of web services in computer networks. Its purpose is to provide extensibility, neutrality, verbosity and independence. [vague] It uses XML Information Set for its message format, and relies on application layer protocols, most often Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP), although some legacy systems communicate over Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP), for message negotiation and transmission. SOAP allows developers to invoke processes running on disparate operating systems (such as Windows, macOS, and Linux) to authenticate, authorize, and communicate using Extensible Markup Language (XML). Since Web protocols like HTTP are installed and running on all operating systems, SOAP allows clients to invoke web services and receive responses independent of language and platforms.

glossary.name: Spines
glossary.description: Switches that connect leaf switches. The spine layer is the backbone of the network and is responsible for interconnecting all leaf switches.

glossary.name: TACACS+
glossary.description: Terminal Access Controller Access-Control System Plus (TACACS+) is a protocol developed by Cisco and released as an open standard beginning in 1993. Although derived from TACACS, TACACS+ is a separate protocol that handles authentication, authorization, and accounting (AAA) services.

glossary.name: Talos
glossary.description: Cisco Talos Intelligence Group is one of the largest commercial threat intelligence teams in the world., Comprised of world-class cyber security researchers. For more information, see Talos Intelligence.

glossary.name: Tech Preview
glossary.description: A stage of the Cisco product launch process in which the technology is previewed while under development. It precedes the General Availability stage.

glossary.name: Tenant
glossary.description: A tenant is a logical container for application policies that enable an administrator to exercise domain-based access control. Tenants can represent a customer in a service provider setting, an organization or domain in an enterprise setting, or just a convenient grouping of policies. For more information, see What are Tenants?.

glossary.name: Terraform
glossary.description: Terraform is an open-source infrastructure as code software tool created by HashiCorp. Users define and provision data center infrastructure using a declarative configuration language known as HashiCorp Configuration Language, or optionally JSON.

glossary.name: Tetration
glossary.description: In mathematics, tetration (or hyper-4) is an operation based on iterated, or repeated, exponentiation. It is the next hyperoperation after exponentiation, but before pentation. The word was coined by Reuben Louis Goodstein from tetra- (four) and iteration.

glossary.name: TGW
glossary.description: Transit gateway is a network transit hub used to interconnect your virtual private clouds (VPCs) and on-premises networks.

glossary.name: TLS
glossary.description: Transport Layer Security (TLS), and its now-deprecated predecessor, Secure Sockets Layer (SSL), are cryptographic protocols designed to provide communications security over a computer network. Several versions of the protocols are widely used in applications such as web browsing, email, instant messaging, and voice over IP (VoIP). Websites can use TLS to secure all communications between their servers and web browsers.

glossary.name: Topology
glossary.description: Network topology is the arrangement of the elements (links, nodes, etc.) of a communication network. Network topology can be used to define or describe the arrangement of various types of telecommunication networks, including command and control radio networks, industrial field busses and computer networks.

glossary.name: ToR hardware
glossary.description: Tor is short for The Onion Router and was initially a worldwide network of servers developed with the U.S. Navy that enabled people to browse the internet anonymously. The Tor network disguises your identity by encrypting your traffic and moving it across different Tor relays within the network.

glossary.name: UCS Director
glossary.description: Cisco UCS Director is a complete, highly secure, end-to-end management, orchestration, and automation solution for a wide array of Cisco and non-Cisco data infrastructure components, and for industry leading converged infrastructure solutions based on the Cisco UCS and Cisco Nexus platforms. For more information, see Cisco UCS Director Administration Guide.

glossary.name: UCS Manager
glossary.description: A standalone product. Cisco UCS® Manager provides unified, embedded management of all software and hardware components of the Cisco Unified Computing System™ (Cisco UCS) and Cisco HyperFlex™ Systems across multiple chassis and rack servers and thousands of virtual machines. It supports all Cisco UCS product models, including Cisco UCS B-Series Blade Servers and C-Series Rack Servers, Cisco UCS S-Series Storage Servers, Cisco UCS Mini, and Cisco HyperFlex hyperconverged infrastructure, as well as the associated storage resources and networks. Cisco UCS Manager is embedded on a pair of Cisco UCS 6400, 6300 or 6200 Series Fabric Interconnects (FIs) using a clustered, active-standby configuration for high availability. The manager participates in server device discovery, inventory, configuration, provisioning, diagnostics, monitoring, fault detection, auditing, and statistics collection. For more information, see Cisco UCS Manager Data Sheet.

glossary.name: UCSM
glossary.description: Cisco Unified Computing System Manager provides unified management of all hardware and software components for the Cisco UCS solution. UCSM also provides both graphical and command line user interfaces and is embedded into Fabric Interconnects (FIs).

glossary.name: UDI
glossary.description: Unique Device Identifier. The Unique Device Identifier (UDI) is the Cisco Systems product identification standard for hardware products. For more information, see Product Identification Standard.

glossary.name: UEFI Secure Boot
glossary.description: A feature within HyperFlex. Unified Extensible Firmware Interface (UEFI) is a specification that defines a software interface between an operating system and platform firmware. HyperFlex Data Platform uses UEFI to replace the BIOS firmware interfaces. This allows the BIOS to run in UEFI mode while still providing legacy support.

glossary.name: Umbrella SIG
glossary.description: Umbrella is a Cisco cloud-based Secure Internet Gateway (SIG) platform that provides you with multiple levels of defense against internet-based threats. For more information, see Cisco Umbrella SIG User Guide.

glossary.name: Unified Computing System (UCS)
glossary.description: Launched in 2009, Cisco UCS combines servers, networking, and management in a cohesive system where form factor is irrelevant. You choose what is best for your application: blade, rack, storage, or multi-node server. This common operating environment enables optimization for traditional workloads, data analytics, and cloud-native applications with open APIs for broad interoperability and unparalleled automation. For more information, see Modernizing computing infrastructure e-book.

glossary.name: Validated Designs
glossary.description: Cisco Validated Designs (CVDs) provide the foundation for systems design. They are based on common use cases or engineering system priorities.

glossary.name: vBlock
glossary.description: Combine VMware vSphere software running on Cisco Unified Computing Systems (UCS) connected with Cisco Nexus switches, attached to EMC Symmetrix storage. Despite the block in the name, the storage can be accessed as either a block device or a file server.

glossary.name: vCenter
glossary.description: vCenter Server is the centralized management utility for VMware, and is used to manage virtual machines, multiple ESXi hosts, and all dependent components from a single centralized location.

glossary.name: VIB
glossary.description: vSphere Installation Bundle (VIB) is similar to a tarball or ZIP archive. It is a collection of files packaged into a single archive to facilitate distribution.

glossary.name: vManage
glossary.description: The vManage NMS is a centralized network management system that provides a GUI interface to easily monitor, configure, and maintain all Viptela devices and links in the overlay network. The vManage NMS software runs on a server in the network.

glossary.name: VMRC Tunneling
glossary.description: Virtual Machine Remote Console Tunneling

glossary.name: VMware vLCM
glossary.description: vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) is a software that is supported in vSphere 7.0 and later versions. vLCM can be enabled per cluster and allows coordinating the software updates with firmware through a Hardware Support Manager(HSM).

glossary.name: VMware vSphere/ESXi
glossary.description: VMware operating system

glossary.name: vND
glossary.description: Virtual Network Device is designed to create virtual switches, hubs, links, access points and WICs for interconnecting virtual machines. vND runs entirely in user space, and it can use TCP or UDP sockets as well as Linux local sockets, taps and VDEs to interconnect with the emulators of the virtual machines. software that emulates basic layer 1/2 network components to build virtual networks.

glossary.name: VNet Peering
glossary.description: VNet peering is a mechanism that connects two virtual networks (VNets) in the same region through the Azure backbone network. Once peered, the two virtual networks appear as one for all connectivity purposes.

glossary.name: vPC
glossary.description: A virtual Port Channel (vPC) allows links that are physically connected to two different Cisco Nexus™ 5000 Series devices to appear as a single Port Channel to a third device. The third device can be a Cisco Nexus 2000 Series Fabric Extender or a switch, server, or any other networking device.

glossary.name: VROC
glossary.description: Virtual RAID on CPU. Intel VROC is a part of Intel RSTe. This was mostly designed with NVMe SSD in mind and it is directly attached to the Intel Xeon Scalable processors. For the full functionality it uses a newer Intel technology called Intel VMD (Intel Volume Management Device).

glossary.name: vWire
glossary.description: Virtual wires bind two interfaces within a firewall, allowing you to easily install a firewall into a topology that requires no switching or routing by those interfaces. In a virtual wire deployment, you install a firewall transparently on a network segment by binding two firewall ports (interfaces) together.

glossary.name: VXLAN
glossary.description: VXLAN is an encapsulation protocol that provides data center connectivity using tunneling to stretch Layer 2 connections over an underlying Layer 3 network. In data centers, VXLAN is the most commonly used protocol to create overlay networks that sit on top of the physical network, enabling the use of virtual networks.

glossary.name: XTACACS
glossary.description: Extended TACACS (XTACACS) is a proprietary extension to TACACS introduced by Cisco Systems in 1990 without backwards compatibility to the original protocol. TACACS and XTACACS both allow a remote access server to communicate with an authentication server in order to determine if the user has access to the network.

data.question: What is Cisco Intersight?
data.answer: Cisco Intersight™ is a management platform delivered as a service with embedded analytics for your Cisco and 3rd party IT infrastructure. This platform offers an intelligent level of management that enables IT organizations to analyze, simplify, and automate their environments in more advanced ways than the prior generations of tools.

data.question: What do I need to get started with Cisco Intersight?
data.answer: You need a valid Cisco ID, an Intersight account, and at least one qualifying target.

data.question: Where do I create a Cisco ID, if I don’t have one?
data.answer: You can create a Cisco ID at Cisco.com.

data.question: How do I create an Intersight account?
data.answer: Click the Create an Account link on Intersight.com, sign in with your Cisco ID, and claim a target. For more information about how to claim a target, see Claim a Target.

data.question: How do I create multiple Intersight accounts for the same Cisco ID?
data.answer: You can create multiple accounts using the same Cisco ID. Use the Create an Account link on intersight.com and follow the same process used to create an Intersight account. For more information about how to claim a target, see Claim a Target.

data.question: How do I recover an Intersight account if the identity provider (IdP) is not working or an Account Administrator is not available?
data.answer: In some cases, Cisco may not be able to recover access, or it may take considerable time. If no other Account Administrators exist, contact Cisco TAC team. For security purposes, your identity may need to be validated before taking action.To reduce user management and minimize lock-out issues, it is recommended to have multiple Account Administrator users across multiple IdPs (preferably 1 being Cisco ID). Learn more about adding users and adding IdPs.

data.question: How can I directly navigate to my identity provider for authentication?
data.answer: You can bypass the Intersight main page during login by using a link containing the Intersight account and SSO ID, which takes take you directly to your identity provider for authentication. The link should be structured to include the Account ID, as well as the user ID being authenticated. An example of the link is: https://5eb2e1e47564612d3079fe92.intersight.com/iam/weblogin?email=abc@domain.com.

data.question: How do I claim a system into Intersight?
data.answer: Enter the Device ID and Claim Code on the Account Creation details page to claim a system into Intersight.

data.question: How do I claim multiple targets at once in Intersight? Can I automate the bulk target claim process?
data.answer: You can automate bulk target claim using the Cisco Intersight API and Python SDK. For more information, see Automated Intersight Device Claim.

data.question: Do I need different Intersight accounts for different platform types? (Ex: Cisco UCS Servers and Cisco HyperFlex)
data.answer: No, you do not. A single Intersight account displays all platform types depending on your license.

data.question: I have lost my Cisco Intersight account URL. How do I recover it?
data.answer: Please contact the individual who set up the Intersight account for your organization. As the account owner, they can log in directly from intersight.com and provide the Account ID or URL (account_id.intersight.com) to you. The Intersight Account ID is located in the Profile menu.

data.question: What terms and conditions apply to Intersight?
data.answer: Use of Intersight is governed by Cisco General Terms and applicable Product Specific Terms, and they are collectively referred to as General Terms.

data.question: Where can I find the Intersight Universal Cloud Agreement (UCA)?
data.answer: Cisco's updated Cisco General Terms contains the terms of use applicable to all Cisco software (on-premises and cloud-hosted). We no longer require an End User License Agreement (EULA) for on-premise software and a Universal Cloud Agreement (UCA) for cloud-hosted software.

data.question: What are some of the best practices for Intersight account access control?
data.answer:
• Each account should have multiple Account Administrator users to ensure continuity, in the event an Account Administrator loses access to their account or is otherwise unavailable.

  • Each account should have at least one Cisco CCO ID configured, even if SAML/IdP is configured for access.

    • This enables users to access their account in cases when there are configuration issues that prevent logging in to Intersight using SSO.

  • You can choose to use a Service Account as an Account Administrator instead of using a personal account. Activate Multi-Factor Authentication along with your Cisco ID for Intersight account access.

data.question: What is a Beta release?
data.answer:
A Beta release provides early access to new features, typically as part of a public preview. The purpose of a Beta release is to evaluate the usability and stability of the feature, gather user feedback, and help ensure the feature is complete and offers a positive user experience before general availability. Beta features are intended for use in test environments only, not in production or with live data. Cisco encourages users to try Beta features as part of their normal workflows and to share performance and usability feedback through Intersight feedback.
Note: Features previously labeled as Preview have been renamed as Beta releases. If you previously participated in a Preview feature, you will now see these features referred to as Beta. No other changes have been made to their functionality or availability.

data.question: How do I unclaim a target managed by Intersight?
data.answer: You can unclaim a target from the Intersight UI or from the Device Connector at the endpoint. To delete a target from the UI, go to Targets, select the target that you want to delete, and click the Trash can icon. To unclaim a target from the Device connector, log in to the target, navigate to the Device Connector, and click Unclaim. For more information about Unclaiming a target and its impact, see Unclaim Targets.

data.question: How do I delete an Intersight Account?
data.answer:
Warning: Data cannot be recovered from a deleted account.

If you are an Account Administrator of the Intersight Account, then you can delete the account using the Delete a 'iam.Account' resource API. Do the following:

  • Login to the Intersight Account that needs to be deleted, and note down the Account ID.

  • Use the DELETE with Account ID API, accessible at Intersight API Reference, to delete the Account.

  • To verify, logout from the Intersight account and ensure it is not listed under a Cisco or User account.

data.question: What happens to my data after I delete my account?
data.answer: After account deletion, data is retained for a period of 90 days. You can place a request to purge the data upon termination of service. See the Data deletion and retention section in the Intersight Privacy Data Sheet​.

questions.group: Browser Support, Supported Systems, and Multi-Language Support

data.question: What are the supported browsers for Intersight?
data.answer:
For the best experience on https://Intersight.com, we suggest using the most recent version of one of the following browsers:

  • Google Chrome

  • Microsoft Edge

  • Mozilla Firefox

  • Apple Safari

data.question: What is required to make existing Cisco UCS and HyperFlex Systems Intersight ready?
data.answer: You must run the supported software versions of Cisco UCS Manager 3.2(1) and later, Cisco IMC 3.1(1) and later, HyperFlex Connect 2.5(1) and later.

data.question: Which systems does Cisco Intersight Support?
data.answer: For detailed information about Supported Systems, see Supported Systems.

data.question: Is multi-language support available in Intersight?
data.answer: Yes, the Intersight UI currently supports Traditional Chinese, Japanese, and Korean languages.

data.question: How do I toggle between the languages?
data.answer: On intersight.com, click the globe icon and select the language that you want to switch to. You can also change the language preferences after you login to Intersight, from Profile > User Preferences > Select language.

data.question: Does Intersight use HTML5 on all pages? Does Intersight require any additional plugins?
data.answer: Yes, Intersight uses HTML5 on all pages. Intersight does not require any additional plugins beyond your browser's capabilities.

data.question: What License tiers are available for Intersight?
data.answer:
Cisco Intersight has the following license tiers:
- Cisco Intersight Infrastructure Services - Essentials and Advantage license tiers.
- Cisco Workload Optimizer Essentials, Advantage, and Premier license tiers

data.question: How do I buy a new Cisco Intersight license, or renew my existing license?
data.answer: For details about how to purchase, or renew a Cisco Intersight license, reach out to the Cisco Sales representative, channel partner, or reseller.

data.question: What features are included in the Cisco Infrastructure Services - Essentials and Advantage edition?
data.answer:
The Essentials license tier offers server management with global health monitoring, inventory, proactive support through Cisco TAC integration, multi-factor authentication, along with SDK and API access.
The Advantage license tier offers advanced server management with extended visibility, ecosystem integration, and automation of Cisco and third-party hardware and software, along with multi-domain solutions.
For information on the features and tiers of the Cisco Infrastructure Services license, see License Requirements.

data.question: What new features are included in the Cisco Workload Optimizer Essentials, Advantage, and Premier edition?
data.answer: Cisco Intersight Workload Optimizer uses a subscription-based license with multiple tiers. You can purchase a subscription duration of one, three, or five years and choose the tier they need for the selected subscription duration. The unit of measure is the number of virtual machines instances or number of virtual desktop instances. For more information, see Workload Optimizer Licensing.

data.question: How do I get a free trial?
data.answer: Create an account, go to the licensing page, click Start Trial.

data.question: How long is the trial period?
data.answer: The trial period is 90 days for Intersight and 45 days for Workload Optimizer.

data.question: How do I claim different servers under different license types in the same Intersight account?
data.answer: Intersight provides the ability to have multiple active license tiers within a single Intersight account. With multi-tier licensing, you can assign servers to a preferred tier giving you additional flexibility to apply the right level of features to specific systems. For more information, see Multiple licensing tiers in Intersight.

data.question: What happens when my free trial expires?
data.answer: When the trial expires, functionality is downgraded to None (Not Licensed Servers) and features that require a license will become unavailable. Your configuration will be retained, but you will not be able to fully access those features until you acquire proper licensing.

data.question: Why is my license showing an out-of-compliance status?
data.answer: Your license could move to an out-of-compliance status if you exceed the number of entitled licenses or the subscribed term, or if the grace period expires. To learn specific details about why your license is not in compliance, see Cisco Smart Software Licensing.

data.question: Are there any limitations to the number of servers I can register during the grace period?
data.answer: No. During the grace period you can use the full entitled number of servers.

data.question: Does my application traffic flow into Intersight?
data.answer: All application data and traffic remain on premises and control data (configuration, provisioning, and monitoring) is transmitted to Intersight.

data.question: Do I need to open ports on my firewall to manage targets with Cisco Intersight?
data.answer:
All device connectors must properly resolve svc.intersight.com or svc.eu-central-1.intersight.com and allow outbound initiated HTTPS connections on port 443. To resolve svc.intersight.com, you must configure DNS on the managed targets. If a proxy is required for an HTTPS connection to svc.intersight.com, the proxy can be configured in the device connector user interface. In addition, to leverage certificate validation, open port 80 and allow communication to amazontrust.com in your firewall settings. Allowing for certificate validation is optional but recommended. For more information, see Network Connectivity Requirements​.

Note: Security appliances that terminate outbound device connector HTTPS connections are not supported at this time.

data.question: Does Cisco have access to data running in my private cloud?
data.answer: No, Cisco Intersight connects to the platform management interface and the host/application data is not shared or accessed by Cisco Intersight.

data.question: How often is target information sent to the Intersight portal?
data.answer: Inventory is updated 24 hours, and faults are updated based on an event subscription to the endpoint. Target information is also refreshed in response to various operations initiated from Cisco Intersight. For example, the Power Off (or a similar) operation will refresh the power state.

data.question: What security measures are in-place to securely store collected information?
data.answer: Intersight adopts technical and organizational security measures as required by law and by industry standards to protect your personal data. For more information, see the Personal data security section, in the Intersight Privacy Datasheet. For an overview of security in the Cisco Intersight Platform Solution, see the information here.

data.question: Does Cisco Intersight support Single Sign-on (SSO) to integrate with my company’s directory services?
data.answer: Yes, Intersight supports SSO via SAML 2.0 to enable you to integrate with your company's directory services. For more FAQs on SSO with Intersight, see the Single-Sign on section.

data.question: How do I restore a lost target connection?
data.answer:
To restore a lost target connection, check for the following in the Device Connector UI in Cisco UCS Manager, Cisco IMC, Cisco HyperFlex, or Cisco UCS Director:

  • A network connection to the Intersight platform is established from the Device Connector in the endpoint.

  • Intersight Management is enabled in the Device Connector. You can enable it from Admin > Device Connector > Intersight Management in Cisco UCS Manager/Cisco UCS Director/Cisco IMC, and Settings > Device Connector in the Cisco HyperFlex UI.

  • A valid CA-signed certificate presented by the Intersight portal exists.

  • DNS is configured and the DNS name is resolved.

  • NTP is configured.

  • Check if a firewall is introduced between the managed target and Intersight, or if the rules for an existing firewall have changed, thus affecting connectivity. If the rules have changed, ensure that they permit traffic through the firewall.

  • If you use a HTTP proxy to route traffic out of your premises, and if you have made changes to the HTTP proxy server's configuration, ensure that you change the Device Connector's configuration accordingly. This is required because Intersight does not automatically detect HTTP proxy servers.

After you ensure that the items listed above are in place, you can log in to the managed target and navigate to the Device Connector Admin page. From the Admin page, you can find details about the specific connectivity error as observed by the Device Connector. For more information about locating the Device Connector Admin page, see the Cisco Intersight Setup section in intersight.com/getting_started

data.question: How often is the Intersight customer data backed up?
data.answer: Customer data is encrypted using industry-standard AES-256 algorithm and backed up daily using automated scripts. Backups are replicated to a second region. Intersight retains the last 30 daily backups.

data.question: What is a Resource Group in Intersight?
data.answer: A Resource Group is a logical container of resources available for a user account. An Intersight user can classify and manage the resources through resource groups. The user can assign the resource groups to one or multiple organizations.

data.question: What is an Organization in Intersight?
data.answer: An Organization is a logical entity that supports multi-tenancy within an account through grouping of resources. An Intersight user can map the resource group to specific organizations and configure profiles and policies within the organization that can be applied to the targets.

data.question: What is a Role? What is a system-defined role?
data.answer: A role represents a collection of privileges to perform a set of operations and provides a user access to resources. A system-defined role is created by default in an account. Each system-defined role has a unique privilege such as a Device Administrator or Service Administrator privilege associated with it. These roles cannot be edited by the users and they apply to the entire account. The system-defined roles in Intersight are Account Administrator, Read-Only, HyperFlex Cluster Administrator, Server Administrator, User Access Administrator, Device Administrator, and Device Technician.

data.question: What is a user-defined role? Why do I need a user-defined role?
data.answer: User-defined roles are introduced to grant multiple privileges to a role, and to expand the scope of a privilege to the whole account or an Organization. For example, 'Role-All' could include a combination of Server Administrator and Device Administrator privileges applicable account-wide. Role-Org - 'Org1' could include a combination of Server Administrator and Read-Only privileges, and 'Org2' could include a combination of HyperFlex Cluster Administrator and Server Administrator privileges, applicable organization-wide.

data.question: What is a privilege?
data.answer: A Privilege is a set of actions grouped by functionality, such as Server Administrator, Read-Only, Device Technician etc. A role can have a unique or multiple privileges associated with it.

data.question: What can I do after Target Claim to leverage custom organizations to manage access to my infrastructure?
data.answer: By default, an organization with name default is created and the newly added Targets are placed in this default organization. A User in a default Organization has access to all the targets available for the User Account. Any new targets claimed will be added to this default organization. Account Administrators have the option to disable this setting to automatically add new targets to default organization. They can also rename or delete the default organization provided there is at least one organization after the deletion. Navigate to Settings > ACCESS & PERMISSIONS > Organizations, to edit the default organization and move targets to a custom organization.

data.question: How long does it take for all my targets to be placed in a newly created organization?
data.answer: Depending on the number of targets in the organization, it could take a few minutes to update the target inventory.

data.question: Who can create or delete an Organization?
data.answer: Only an Account Administrator can create or delete an Organization.

data.question: Who can create user-defined roles?
data.answer: A user with Account Administrator or User Access Administrator privileges can create user-defined roles.

data.question: How do I know what organization(s) I have access to and the privileges I have in each organization?
data.answer: All existing targets and policies and profiles are placed in a default organization. When you use Organizations to leverage multi-tenancy, you can view the details of your Organization in Settings > General> Access Details.

data.question: How do I access details about the organizations in my account?
data.answer: You must have an Account Administrator role. You can view the details of your Organization in Settings > Access & Permissions > Organizations.

data.question: What happens when I move between organizations within a company?
data.answer: When you move between organizations in a company (for example, from Marketing to HR), depending on how you log in to Intersight, the IdP administrator or the Intersight Account Administrator must make the changes in the IdP application or in Intersight respectively. If you log in to Intersight through a user group, the IdP administrator must change your membership from one user group to another. If you are added directly to Intersight, then the Account Administrator must change your role and privileges to adhere to the new organization.

data.question: Are there any restrictions or limits on the maximum number of roles and organizations a user can be associated with?
data.answer: For detailed information about the maximum limits on the number of roles and organizations, see Guidelines and Limitations.

data.question: Can a user in a group have multiple roles in different organizations? Are there any restrictions on the number of roles he can hold?
data.answer: Yes, a user or a user group can have a maximum of 50 roles mapped to different organizations. All users in a group will have the same roles that are mapped to the group.

data.question: What is Single Sign-On?
data.answer: Single Sign-On (SSO) authentication enables you to use a single set of credentials to log into multiple applications. With SSO, you can sign into Cisco Intersight with your corporate credentials instead of your Cisco ID.

data.question: How does Intersight enable SSO?
data.answer: Cisco Intersight supports SSO via SAML 2.0 and acts as a Service Provider (SP) to enable integration with IdPs for SSO authentication. You can configure your account to use the Cisco ID and SSO to sign into Intersight. In order to integrate your IdP with Intersight, you must have an Intersight account. If you do not have an Intersight account, you can set up one on intersight.com.

data.question: Why am I seeing two different options to log into Intersight?
data.answer: Intersight now provides two options to sign in. Select Cisco ID, or your corporate ID that you have registered with your IdP if you want to sign in with SSO. You can sign into your account from https://intersight.com/ or log in directly with your account URL which is https://account_id.intersight.com.

data.question: Where do I see my account details after I log into Intersight?
data.answer: From the Settings drop-down menu, select Settings to view the account details, including Account Name, Account ID and the Access Link.

data.question: Can I add, modify, or delete a user account in Intersight?
data.answer: Yes, if you are a user with an Account Administrator role, you can add, modify, delete other accounts including other admin accounts.

data.question: Will my Intersight session be removed when I am inactive for a period of time?
data.answer: Yes, if your session is idle for more than 30 minutes, your session will be removed.

data.question: What IdPs have been validated by Intersight?
data.answer: For the latest list of IdPs validated by Intersight, see Supported Systems.​ For more information on the SAML support provided by the Intersight SP, see Single Sign-On with Intersight.

data.question: Can I switch between two accounts without logging out?
data.answer: No, you must log out and log in back again with the correct account ID/URL.

data.question: What is Multi-Factor Authentication?
data.answer: Multi-Factor Authentication is the process of using an additional factor to identify users, in addition to the password or another known factor. Multi-Factor Authentication provides an extra layer of security to protect your account and reduces the risk of unauthorized access to your sensitive data.

data.question: When will I be required to set up Multi-Factor Authentication?
data.answer: You will be required to set it up for your new or existing Intersight account after you enable the requirement for Multi-Factor Authentication in Intersight.

data.question: Is the verification code mandatory?
data.answer: Yes, the verification code is mandatory when you enable Multi-Factor Authentication, regardless of where you enable it from - Intersight or from the Cisco ID Profile Management portal.

data.question: How can I generate a verification code?
data.answer: You can install a desktop/mobile app or choose to get the verification code sent to your email. Follow the instructions provided in the Cisco ID Profile Management portal to complete the setup of the authentication application before you generate the verification code.

data.question: How do I reuse an account that I created in an app to regenerate the verification code when Multi-Factor Authentication is disabled and re-enabled?
data.answer: You can reuse the verification code generated by the app only until you enable or disable the requirement for Multi-Factor Authentication from Intersight account settings. Once you disable Multi-Factor Authentication in the Cisco ID Profile Management portal, the verification code generated by the existing app account becomes invalid. You must scan the QR code and recreate an app account.

data.question: How do I switch between user accounts where one of the accounts is Multi-Factor Authentication enabled?
data.answer: You must setup Multi-Factor Authentication in the Cisco ID Profile Management portal for the account for which the requirement for Multi-Factor Authentication is enabled. Follow the procedure in the Intersight portal to set up Multi-Factor Authentication.

questions.group: Automatic Generation of Tech Support Files by Cisco TAC

data.question: How do I enable this feature?
data.answer: You can enable this feature by creating an Intersight account, accepting the Cisco General Terms, and claiming a target with your Intersight account.

data.question: Do I need to purchase a license to take advantage of this?
data.answer: No, you do not need any additional license to take advantage of this feature. It is included with the hardware that you purchase from Cisco and is available to use when you access the Intersight portal and claim a device.

data.question: Can I see the files that are uploaded?
data.answer: No, tech Support files are generated locally at the endpoints, and are available only to Cisco TAC engineers who own the case.

data.question: Can I be notified when a Tech Support file is automatically generated?
data.answer: Yes. When Tech Support files are collected, they are attached as external notes in the Cisco Support Case Manager (SCM) tool. You can configure the e-mail contact in the SCM preferences and will receive notifications when the case is updated, including when a Tech Support file has been attached to the case.

data.question: Who is authorized to collect Tech Support files?
data.answer: Only registered Intersight users and Cisco engineers who are working on a case are authorized to collect Tech Support files.

data.question: Can I get additional information on a device from Cisco TAC?
data.answer:
Yes, however, you must have Account Administrator privilege to get additional information on a device from Cisco TAC.

  • Information about the Intersight user who claimed a given target can be obtained from the Device Connector. For more information, see Configuring Device Connector​.

  • Targets claimed under an account can be viewed from ADMIN > Targets within Intersight. For more information, see Claim Targets​

data.question: Will Cisco make changes to my Intersight account or devices connected to it?
data.answer: No, Cisco will not perform device or account configuration changes on your behalf.

data.question: What is the difference between Intersight and Intersight Virtualization Service?
data.answer: Intersight is a platform that offers a host of functionalities related to IIS (infrastructure management), IWO (workload optimization), ICO (orchestration), IST (Terraform support), and Virtualization management. Intersight Virtualization Service is a component of Intersight that provides virtualization management for hybrid clouds. Learn more about Intersight on the Overview ​page.

data.question: How do I connect my VMware cloud to Intersight?
data.answer: Refer to Claim Targets.

data.question: What are the licensing requirements for the Intersight Virtualization Service?
data.answer: Virtualization inventory and Virtualization operation features within Intersight are available for users with Intersight Advantage license. You need at least one server with Advantage license to be able to use Intersight Virtualization Service. For more information, see Licensing Requirements.

data.question: I have Cisco IWO licenses, so will I be able to access Intersight Virtualization Services?
data.answer: Although you may have Cisco IWO licenses, you will need at least one server with Intersight Advantage license to use Virtualization features. For more information, see Licensing Requirements.

data.question: How do I get my trial licenses to try IVS?
data.answer: Please refer to the FAQs page.

data.question: I already use Intersight Cloud Orchestrator (ICO), what is the benefit of using IVS?
data.answer: IVS provides a more intuitive way to manage virtualization infrastructure by allowing users to view the virtualization inventory and initiate supported operations via the user interface.

data.question: Can I perform Application blueprint/Resource profiles/Templates using IVS?
data.answer: IVS does not support these features.

data.question: Why does the server profile show the status as out-of-sync?
data.answer: The out-of-sync status indicates that the configuration of a policy at the endpoint is not in sync with the configuration of the policy deployed on the server profile. This status is displayed as a part of the Configuration Drift support for servers. For more information see Server Profiles.

data.question: What license is required to access Intersight API?
data.answer: Intersight API requires an Intersight Essentials license.

data.question: How do I access the Intersight API?
data.answer: The Intersight API is available at intersight.com/​apidocs​.

data.question: How many servers can I connect to an account in Intersight?
data.answer: There is no limit to the number of targets you can connect in a single account in Intersight. However, time taken to complete registration of your targets will depend on the number of systems you want to concurrently register.

data.question: Can Intersight co-exist with Cisco UCS Manager, Cisco UCS Central, Cisco IMC, IMC Supervisor, Cisco UCS Director, and Cisco HyperFlex Connect?
data.answer: Cisco Intersight can co-exist with existing system management tools, but you should be careful when performing platform operations such as server configuration or firmware upgrades, since these can be initiated from multiple systems in parallel. As a best practice, there should be an approved process and flow associated with change management where the systems let these operations be initiated without impedance.

data.question: How is the device connector upgraded?
data.answer:
Device connectors are upgraded automatically without any impact on other software components. They are upgraded in the following cases:

  • When a supported system (Cisco UCS Manager, Cisco HyperFlex) is upgraded, except when the device connector is already at a recent version.

  • When security patches are required.

  • When a new functionality introduced in Intersight requires a device connector upgrade.

data.question: What information does the Device Connector collect?
data.answer:
Device Connectors collect the following information:

  • Inventory data for all components including storage controllers, network adapters, IO modules, Fabric Interconnects, and CPUs for Cisco UCS and HyperFlex Systems.

  • Inventory, and Server Operational data, such as faults to provide automated recommendations.

  • Tech Support files when requested by Cisco Technical Assistance.

Note: Device Connectors do not collect sensitive data such as passwords configured in the supported systems.

data.question: Who has access to information collected by Intersight?
data.answer: The Cisco Intersight supported team, a limited group of Cisco engineers and support staff have access to information collected by Intersight. See Access control in the Intersight Privacy Data Sheet​.

data.question: Why is the device connector screen blank after an upgrade to firmware version 3.0(4) on Cisco UCS C series Standalone M4 servers?
data.answer:
You must upgrade the Cisco IMC software twice in order to enable the Device Connector used with Intersight. You can upgrade using one of the following methods:

  • Automatic double upgrade using the Host Upgrade Utility(HUU).

  • Manually update Cisco IMC firmware twice using the Non-Interactive HUU.

  • Manually update the Cisco IMC firmware using the Cisco IMC Web UI or CLI or XML API interfaces.

data.question: Will the device connector slow down my system?
data.answer: No, the Cisco Intersight Device Connector will not impact host/application performance. This lightweight service runs in the management plane of supported systems.

data.question: How do I update my Intersight version?
data.answer: There are no versions of Intersight. Cisco manages the versions and enables features and functionality periodically. You can access the features based on the License tier you subscribe for.

data.question: How do I make a request for new features/functionality?
data.answer: Within Cisco Intersight, there is an option to provide feedback on desired features. Go to Help > Send Feedback and enter the details.

data.question: What documentation is available?
data.answer: Intersight has in-product guided help to assist you in completing tasks such as Claiming Targets and Adding Users. Additional documentation is available at intersight.com/​help​.
